illustrative.text: Do all students have equitable access to the tools they need to complete the tasks being set?
What role does an Elder, Knowledge Holder, or Métis Senator play in your community?
In what ways might your responsibilities at home, or to the land, change as you get older?
Who were the parties to the Treaty of Niagara or the 1760 Treaty of Peace and Friendship?
What order of importance would you assign to the various factors that led to Confederation?
What do we know about how improved access to education for girls can affect a society?
Why do you think your role at school is different from your role at home?
In what ways do you show that you care for and respect the natural environment?
What are some special memories that you have of spending time with people in your family?
How can families learn about locally harvested foods and recipes that use those foods?
How might you compare your role as a friend to your role as a big sister?
Who might you talk to in order to find out about some of your family’s stories?
Who can help you find some of the information you need?
Where can you go to learn more about your community?
Why might your responsibilities be different in the summer than in the winter?
What events have led to the biggest changes in your sense of self? Why do you think that is?
What roles do you have at school? At home? In the community?
Do you have any pets? If yes, what role do you play in caring for them?
What is your relationship with other members of your family?
What role does your doctor play in your life?
What is the purpose of our school?
What is an object that is special to you? Why is it so special?
Why is it important to treat other people with respect? How can we show respect for others?
How would you feel if the playground were torn down?
How can various people in our community support us in our investigation? Who could we ask?
What happens if the water is not safe to drink, wash, or swim in?
How could we gather data on the messiest places in the schoolyard?
How could we use photos to see where trees have been planted or trees have been cut down?
Let’s look at these old and new pictures of this area of town. What do you see that’s different?
What kinds of buildings do you or your families use? What do you use them for?
Why is blue a good colour to use to show where water is?
What should we use green for?
What would be a good title for a map of the schoolyard?
Why do we label things on maps? How does this help us use a map?
How might you measure the distances between natural and built features on your map?
What types of government and/or community services are found in our community?
Why are services needed in a community? What would happen if we didn’t have these services?
What are some of the roles and responsibilities of the people who work for these services?

Spatial skills underpin spatial literacy, enabling students to develop and communicate a sense of place.
apply these skills in everyday contexts and in other subjects.
Spatial skills are directly linked to literacy, mathematical literacy, and technological skills.
Literacy: Maps, globes, and graphs are graphic text forms, and students need to develop both
language conventions when using other text forms.
Mathematical Literacy: There is a close connection between spatial skills and mathematics. In
mathematics curriculum for Grades 1 to 8: Data, Algebra, and Spatial Sense.
Technology: The social studies, history, and geography curriculum provides many opportunities
the development of both spatial and technological skills.

inquiry process, as the following examples illustrate.
Formulating questions: Students formulate questions related to the type of map or maps best
suited to their inquiry.
Gathering/organizing: Students determine the purpose of different maps and which are most
relevant to their inquiry.
Analysing/interpreting: Students extract information from, plot information on, and/or analyse
various types of maps to help them determine patterns, trends, and/or interrelationships.
Communication: Students construct maps in order to communicate key pieces of information.
